text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY -- ROUTINE FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Patient: Raymond Kowalski | DOB: [on file] | MRN: [on file]
Attending: [Oncologist name on file]
Department: Genitourinary Medical Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Kowalski is a 64-year-old male with metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC) with sarcomatoid differentiation presenting for routine follow-up visit. He is currently on cabozantinib 40 mg orally once daily (second-line; approximately 9 months of TKI therapy). Visit also serves to review recent restaging CT results and assess tolerability of ongoing therapy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski was originally diagnosed approximately 18 months ago with stage IV clear cell RCC (cT3bN1M1), right-sided, presenting with gross hematuria, right flank pain, and 15 lb unintentional weight loss. Initial workup revealed an 11.4 cm right renal mass with renal vein thrombus, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy (right para-aortic and aortocaval nodes), and bilateral pulmonary metastases (largest 1.4 cm RLL). Tumor demonstrated WHO/ISUP grade 4 histology with 22% sarcomatoid differentiation, extensive necrosis, and lymphovascular invasion. Genomic profiling (Foundation One CDx) identified VHL frameshift deletion, co-occurring PBRM1 and BAP1 loss-of-function mutations (prognostically adverse combination associated with higher grade and sarcomatoid features), SETD2 loss, KDM5C mutation, CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion, MET amplification (copy number 6.8), and a TP53 gain-of-function mutation. TMB was low (6.2 mut/Mb), MSS. PD-L1 (22C3) CPS 18.

He underwent cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection approximately 14 months ago. Surgical pathology confirmed R0 resection of the primary; 3/8 retroperitoneal nodes were positive. He then initiated first-line combination immunotherapy with ipilimumab 1 mg/kg + nivolumab 3 mg/kg every 3 weeks for 4 induction cycles, followed by nivolumab 480 mg flat dose every 4 weeks as maintenance. During ipi/nivo induction, he developed grade 2 immune-related hypothyroidism (TSH 18.4, free T4 0.6), managed with levothyroxine without interruption of immunotherapy.

His response to immunotherapy was discordant: pulmonary nodules demonstrated meaningful shrinkage (RLL nodule 1.4 cm to 0.7 cm), but retroperitoneal and pelvic nodal disease progressed consistently over serial scans. By approximately 9 months of total immunotherapy, clear nodal progression was confirmed (right common iliac mass growing to 3.1 cm; aortocaval conglomerate to 3.4 cm; right obturator node appearing and enlarging; new right external iliac node 1.2 cm). Given this nodal progression, nivolumab was discontinued and he was transitioned to second-line cabozantinib, initiated at 60 mg daily. The MET amplification on NGS was specifically highlighted as a potential biomarker of sensitivity to cabozantinib given its activity against the MET receptor tyrosine kinase in addition to VEGFR.

Early in the cabozantinib course, he developed grade 2 hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia) and grade 2 diarrhea approximately 3 weeks in, prompting dose reduction to 40 mg daily. He also developed grade 2 hypertension (BPs 158-162/96-98 on two readings) requiring augmentation of his antihypertensive regimen with addition of amlodipine 5 mg, subsequently uptitrated to 10 mg, to his existing lisinopril 20 mg daily.

Today marks approximately 9 months on cabozantinib. Most recent restaging CT (reviewed at approximately 6 months of TKI) confirmed stable disease: right common iliac mass 2.2 cm (down from 3.1 cm at cabozantinib start; stable from 2.3 cm at first TKI response scan), aortocaval conglomerate 2.5 cm (stable), right obturator node 1.3 cm (stable), right external iliac node 0.8 cm (stable). Pulmonary nodules stable throughout. There was a small 0.5 cm sclerotic focus in the right iliac bone noted on that CT, which prompted a whole-body technetium-99m bone scan; this showed only mildly increased uptake in that region, read as likely reactive or degenerative rather than metastatic, and no other suspicious foci were identified. The bone scan finding was therefore considered reassuring and no further action was taken at that time. Upcoming restaging CT is planned per today's visit.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Reports continued baseline fatigue, grade 1, present for several months, stable and not worsening. Appetite mildly reduced; attributes this to persistent dysgeusia (altered taste, primarily metallic quality) that has been present for several months on cabozantinib. Weight has been stable at approximately 178 lbs as of today's visit (compared with 198 lbs at initial diagnosis, a total loss of approximately 20 lbs over the disease course; however, weight has been stable over the past 3-4 months on cabozantinib). Denies fevers, chills, or night sweats.

Integumentary: Ongoing mild (grade 1) hand-foot syndrome bilaterally -- mild erythema on palms and soles, no active blistering or cracking, no open wounds. Manages with daily urea 20% cream applications morning and night, gel cushion insoles, and avoidance of tight footwear. Denies new rash or significant skin changes elsewhere. No nail changes reported.

Gastrointestinal: Intermittent loose stools, 1-2 above baseline per day (grade 1 diarrhea), managed with dietary modification (low-residue diet) and PRN loperamide approximately 3-4 times per week. No abdominal cramping. No nausea or vomiting currently. No mucositis or oral ulcers. The dysgeusia affects the enjoyment of food but has not caused significant nausea. No rectal bleeding, melena, or hematemesis.

Genitourinary: No hematuria since nephrectomy (resolution of initial presenting symptom). Urinary frequency unchanged from baseline. No pelvic pain or lower urinary tract symptoms. Urine output adequate.

Musculoskeletal: No focal bone pain. No new back pain. No joint swelling. Denies lower extremity edema (resolved after initiating cabozantinib; had been grade 1 at time of transition from nivolumab).

Neurological: No headaches, visual changes, confusion, or focal neurological deficits. Denies symptoms suggestive of CNS metastases.

Cardiovascular/Respiratory: No dyspnea, cough, hemoptysis, or chest pain. No palpitations. Blood pressure management ongoing as above.

Endocrine: Tolerating levothyroxine well. No symptoms of hypothyroidism (fatigue has been stable and attributed to disease/TKI rather than thyroid). No heat intolerance or signs of hyperthyroidism.

Psychosocial: Reports overall acceptable quality of life. Has resumed part-time consultancy work (was a retired industrial engineer, has been doing occasional freelance consulting). Engaged with survivorship counseling. Family supportive. No overt depression or anxiety expressed, though notes ongoing stress related to uncertainty of disease course.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY / ONCOLOGIC HISTORY
(Summarized for completeness at this follow-up visit)
- Metastatic clear cell RCC, stage IV, right kidney, diagnosed approximately 18 months ago (as above)
- Cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with RPLND approximately 14 months ago (R0; ypT3bN1)
- Immune-related hypothyroidism, grade 2, on levothyroxine (stable)
- Hypertension -- pre-existing, managed with lisinopril; now requiring lisinopril 20 mg + amlodipine 10 mg
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus, managed with metformin; blood glucose monitoring ongoing
- Former smoker, 30 pack-year history, quit approximately 15 years prior; COPD not formally diagnosed

ALLERGIES: NKDA (no known drug allergies)

MEDICATIONS:
1. Cabozantinib (Cabometyx) 40 mg orally once daily
2. Levothyroxine (Synthroid) 75 mcg orally once daily (on empty stomach)
3. Lisinopril 20 mg orally once daily
4. Amlodipine 10 mg orally once daily
5. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily with meals
6. Loperamide 2 mg PRN diarrhea (as directed)
7. Urea 20% topical cream BID to hands and feet

SOCIAL HISTORY: Retired industrial engineer, now doing occasional part-time consulting. Lives with his wife. Non-smoker (former smoker, quit ~15 years ago; 30 pack-year history). Denies alcohol excess. No illicit drug use. Maintains light physical activity as tolerated.

FAMILY HISTORY: Father with lung cancer (smoker, deceased). Mother with colon cancer in her 70s. No known family history of renal cell carcinoma, clear cell or otherwise. No known hereditary cancer syndromes.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 134/82 mmHg (well-controlled); heart rate 74 bpm; respiratory rate 16; temperature 98.1°F; SpO2 97% on room air; weight 178.2 lbs (stable).

General: Alert and oriented x3. Well-appearing male, no acute distress. Appears his stated age. Mood appropriate.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Anicteric sclerae. Oral mucosa moist, no mucositis, no oral ulcers. No cervical lymphadenopathy.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1/S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No JVD. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rhonchi, or crackles. No dullness to percussion.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Right subcostal nephrectomy scar well-healed, no signs of hernia. No palpable organomegaly. No palpable mass. Bowel sounds active.

Extremities: No lower extremity pitting edema (resolved). Bilateral hands: mild erythema of thenar eminences and distal finger pads, no fissuring, no blistering (consistent with grade 1 HFS). Bilateral feet: mild erythema of heels and weight-bearing areas of soles, skin somewhat thickened but intact, no open areas (grade 1 HFS). Fingernails and toenails intact without significant changes.

Lymphatics: No palpable cervical, supraclavicular, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy on exam.

Neurological: Alert, cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact, strength 5/5 in all extremities, sensation intact, gait steady.

Performance status: ECOG 1, KPS 80. Patient ambulatory and capable of light work; not fully active but maintaining functional independence.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete blood count: WBC 6.8 x10^3/uL (normal differential); hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL (mild anemia, stable from prior visits, consistent with chronic disease and TKI effect); platelets 312 x10^3/uL (normalized from thrombocytosis at presentation).

Comprehensive metabolic panel: Sodium 138 mEq/L; potassium 4.2 mEq/L; chloride 103 mEq/L; bicarbonate 24 mEq/L; BUN 22 mg/dL; creatinine 1.8 mg/dL (stable, consistent with post-nephrectomy baseline); eGFR approximately 43 mL/min/1.73m2 (CKD stage 3b, stable). Glucose 108 mg/dL (fasting, well-controlled on metformin). Calcium 9.4 mg/dL (normalized from 10.9 mg/dL at diagnosis, no longer elevated).

Liver function tests: AST 24 U/L; ALT 31 U/L; alkaline phosphatase 88 U/L; total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL; albumin 3.8 g/dL. All within normal limits; no hepatotoxicity.

Thyroid function: TSH 2.6 mIU/L (within normal range, well-maintained on current levothyroxine dose). Free T4 1.1 ng/dL (normal).

Lipase: Not checked today; no clinical indication.

HbA1c: 6.8% (good glycemic control on metformin; unchanged from prior).

LDH: 198 U/L (decreased from 287 U/L at diagnosis; trending down over course of treatment -- favorable prognostic signal).

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Most recent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (reviewed at 6-month cabozantinib assessment): Stable disease across all sites. Nodal disease that had progresssed on immunotherapy (right common iliac, aortocaval, right obturator, right external iliac) responded well to cabozantinib initially, with meaningful shrinkage from peak sizes, and has been stable for approximately 3 months on the prior scan. Pulmonary nodules (right lower lobe 0.7 cm, left lower lobe 0.7 cm, right upper lobe 0.3 cm) remain stable throughout. Right iliac bone sclerotic focus (0.5 cm) confirmed to be reactive/degenerative on bone scan. No evidence of hepatic, adrenal, or CNS involvement at any point.

Next restaging CT is due and will be ordered today for scheduling (approximately 3 months since last CT, consistent with our standard q3-month interval on TKI therapy).

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. METASTATIC CLEAR CELL RCC WITH SARCOMATOID DIFFERENTIATION -- SECOND-LINE CABOZANTINIB, ONGOING STABLE DISEASE

Mr. Kowalski has now completed 9 months of cabozantinib 40 mg daily with sustained stable disease across pulmonary and retroperitoneal/pelvic nodal metastatic sites. This represents durable disease control following best partial response in the nodal compartment (peak reduction approximately 3 months into TKI therapy) and has been maintained for approximately 6 months now. We are approaching the median PFS range reported in trials such as METEOR for cabozantinib in the second-line setting, and it is encouraging that he remains on therapy with manageable toxicities and preserved functional status.

Of particular note, the MET amplification identified on his Foundation One CDx panel (copy number 6.8) was considered a potential contributor to the observed TKI response, given cabozantinib's known activity against MET in addition to VEGFR2 and AXL. While we cannot attribute his response definitively to this molecular feature, it remains a hypothesis of interest. The BAP1 loss, associated with higher-grade and sarcomatoid features in his tumor, typically portends a more aggressive clinical course; the fact that he has achieved stable disease for this duration is a reasonably favorable outcome.

Plan:
- Continue cabozantinib 40 mg orally once daily; no dose change indicated given stable disease and acceptable tolerability at current dose.
- Order CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast for restaging (3-month interval from last scan). Also order MRI brain with and without contrast -- while there has been no clinical concern for CNS involvement at any point, we have not performed dedicated brain imaging in some time, and given his disease trajectory and presence of sarcomatoid differentiation, a baseline brain MRI is prudent at this juncture.
- Will also add a serum LDH, CBC, CMP, and thyroid function to standing labs at next visit.
- If stable disease confirmed on upcoming restaging, continue cabozantinib with plan to restage again in 3 months.
- If progression confirmed, will reassess for third-line options including consideration of everolimus, lenvatinib + everolimus combination (approved third-line setting), or belzutifan (HIF-2alpha inhibitor; note: no HIF2A mutation on his NGS, but VHL loss-of-function is the canonical upstream activator of HIF-2alpha signaling, and VHL-altered tumors may still benefit from belzutifan). Clinical trial options will also be explored at time of progression.
- Will maintain close communication with urology/radiation oncology given the nodal-dominant disease pattern; stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT) to oligoprogressive nodes could be considered in the event of isolated nodal progression with systemic stability, if that pattern emerges.

2. HAND-FOOT SYNDROME (GRADE 1, CHRONIC)

Well-managed at grade 1. Patient diligent with topical urea cream BID and protective footwear modifications.
Plan:
- Continue current skin care regimen.
- Reinforce avoidance of prolonged ambulation on hard surfaces, tight shoes.
- If escalation to grade 2 or above, will re-evaluate dose modification (further reduction to 20 mg or treatment interruption per standard algorithms).
- Patient instructed to contact clinic if blistering, cracking, or significant pain recurs.

3. DIARRHEA (GRADE 1, CHRONIC)

Intermittent, manageable with dietary adjustments and PRN loperamide approximately 3-4 times per week. No dehydration, no weight loss attributable to GI toxicity.
Plan:
- Continue low-residue diet counseling.
- Continue PRN loperamide as needed.
- If escalation to grade 2 or above (>4-6 stools above baseline per day or significant functional impairment), will reassess dose modification.
- Ensure adequate oral hydration daily.

4. DYSGEUSIA (GRADE 1, CHRONIC)

Patient has had persistent metallic taste change for several months. This is a recognized class effect of VEGFR TKIs including cabozantinib. Not causing significant weight loss at this time (weight stable).
Plan:
- Counseled on strategies to mitigate taste changes: plastic utensils instead of metal, cold foods, marinating meats, flavor enhancers as tolerated.
- Nutritional supplement drinks (e.g., Ensure) offered as option if oral intake declines.
- Dietitian referral offered; patient declined for now but will reconsider.

5. HYPERTENSION

Blood pressure well-controlled today at 134/82 mmHg on lisinopril 20 mg + amlodipine 10 mg. VEGFR TKI-associated hypertension has been a consistent management challenge but is currently at goal.
Plan:
- Continue current antihypertensive regimen.
- Patient instructed to perform home blood pressure monitoring 2-3 times weekly and contact clinic if readings exceed 150/95 mmHg.
- Will reassess antihypertensive regimen if BP becomes consistently elevated above goal.

6. HYPOTHYROIDISM (IMMUNE-RELATED, GRADE 2 RESOLVED)

TSH 2.6 mIU/L today -- well within normal range on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. No clinical symptoms of thyroid dysfunction.
Plan:
- Continue levothyroxine 75 mcg daily (morning, on empty stomach, 30-60 minutes before food).
- Recheck TSH in 3 months.

7. TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS

HbA1c 6.8% -- good glycemic control on metformin 1000 mg BID. No hypoglycemia. Note: metformin use in setting of CKD stage 3b (eGFR ~43) is acceptable per current ADA guidelines at this eGFR level, but will continue to monitor renal function closely; if eGFR drops below 30, metformin will need to be discontinued.
Plan:
- Continue metformin 1000 mg BID.
- Monitor renal function at each visit.
- Continue home glucose monitoring.

8. ANEMIA (MILD, CHRONIC)

Hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL -- mild, stable anemia consistent with chronic disease process and TKI effect. No symptoms of significant anemia (no dyspnea on exertion, no palpitations).
Plan:
- Monitor on routine labs.
- Iron studies to be checked at next visit to rule out iron deficiency as contributing etiology.
- If hemoglobin drops significantly or becomes symptomatic, will reassess for secondary causes and consider supportive intervention.

9. SURVIVORSHIP / QUALITY OF LIFE

Patient remains engaged and functional at ECOG 1, working part-time, and maintaining social activity. He is now 18 months from diagnosis of stage IV disease -- a significant milestone given the aggressive features of his tumor (grade 4, sarcomatoid, MET-amplified, BAP1/PBRM1 co-deleted, TP53 mutation). Overall trajectory has been favorable relative to initial risk stratification.
Plan:
- Continue survivorship counseling (ongoing).
- Psychological support available PRN.
- Referral to palliative care team for longitudinal symptom management support as appropriate (patient previously declined; readdress at next visit).
- Encourage light physical activity as tolerated; avoid high-impact activities with hands and feet given HFS.

RETURN VISIT: In 3 months, or sooner if new symptoms develop. At that time, restaging CT results (and MRI brain) will be reviewed. Patient instructed to contact clinic for any grade 2 or higher adverse events, new pain, neurological symptoms, blood in urine or stool, or significant change in functional status.

Time spent with patient: approximately 40 minutes (30 minutes clinical evaluation, 10 minutes direct counseling regarding disease status, treatment goals, and toxicity management).

Electronically signed: [Attending Oncologist, MD]
Cosign: [Fellow/APP, as applicable]
